segments.s225-g201: یکی (preposed numeral/quantifier) qualifies پیلبازی and here means ‘one’ / ‘a single’ (a single performance). پیلبازی is a compound noun ‘elephant-display’ and functions as the direct object of نمایم. نمایم is the 1st-person singular present (classical/persian -am) of نمود/نمودن ‘to show’ — in older Persian it expresses the speaker’s intention/near future ‘I will show / I will perform.’ بدوی is an adverbial/qualifying word (archaic/poetic) modifying نمایم (manner or timing of the showing).
segments.s225-g202: کزان is a compound relative/prepositional particle ‘from that / from which’ introducing the result clause. پس here means ‘after that/thereupon’ and links the consequence to the preceding action. نیارد is a poetic/archaic 3rd-person singular form meaning ‘(he) does not/will not bring/does not cause’ (negative of آوردن in older spelling), placed before the directional phrase. سوی جنگ روی is a prepositional/directional phrase: سوی ‘toward’, جنگ ‘the battle/war’, and روی ‘face’ used with روی آوردن (to turn one’s face) — together ‘turn his face toward the battle.’
segments.s225-g57: گروی is an archaic/poetic 3rd‑person verb form meaning ‘took up / adopted’ (literally ‘he adopted’); its object زره (armor) follows it in inverted poetic order (verb before object). آنک ... بزاد begins the relative clause that follows 'and' (و).
segments.s225-g58: نژادی is a noun meaning ‘a lineage / race’; that introduces a relative clause with که. هرگز مباد is a negative jussive formula: هرگز ‘never’ + مباد ‘may it not be / may it never occur’ — here wishing that the described lineage never be (or never should come to pass). آن نژاد repeats/points to 'that race' (demonstrative).
segments.s225-g59: This clause has the standard order: ستم (oppression) as subject, بر سیاوش indicating the target ('against Siavash'), از ایشان (here orthographically ازیشان) showing the source ('from them'), and رسید as the past tense verb 'reached/came to'.
segments.s225-g60: که introduces a consequential clause: ز (poetic form of از) و آمد 'from him came' (ز + و + آمد = 'from him came'). این بند بد را کلید is a noun phrase with را marking the definite object: 'the key of this bad (evil) bond' (بند = fetter/chain, بد = evil/bad, کلید = key, here figurative).
segments.s225-g61: کسی کو is an archaic construction meaning roughly 'one whom/one who' (کو here is a poetic relative/particle); دل و مغز افراسیاب places the body parts ('heart and brain') linked to the name 'Afrasiab' — i.e. 'whose heart and mind Afrasiab ...' with the verb completing the idea in the next segment.
segments.s225-g62: تبه کرد is a compound of adjective/noun تبه 'wicked' + کرد (past 'made/did') meaning 'made wicked' or 'corrupted'; و خون راند is straightforward: خون 'blood' + راند (past of راندن) 'drove/caused to flow', and بر سان آب is a simile 'as/like water' (بر سان = like, آب = water) meaning he shed blood as freely as water.
segments.s225-g63: و دیگر کسی را کز ایرانیان is a topical phrase: و دیگر 'and another', کسی را (object/topic marker) and کز (= که + ز) ایرانیان 'from among the Iranians' — i.e. 'and another one from the Iranians'.
segments.s225-g64: This clause states a condition/quality: نبد کین = 'there was no grudge/vengeance' (نبد = 'was not/there was not'), و بست اندرین کین میان = 'and (he) bound/closed that grudge among them' — بست is past of بستن 'to bind/close', اندرین کین میان = 'that hatred/grudge among (them)'. The result is that this person had no grudge and acted to seal/stop such enmity among the people.
segments.s225-g89: چنین پاسخ آورد هومان بدوی is a compact clause meaning “Homan answered thus (while running).” چنین modifies پاسخ ('thus/so'), پاسخ is the noun 'answer', and آورد is a 3rd‑person past/punctual verb here meaning 'gave/uttered' (literally 'brought [an answer]'). هومان is the subject/name. بدوی is an adverbial/epithet attached to هومان (here marking his manner or epithet 'rushing/wild' in the poem) and functions like a trailing participial/epithetic word describing Homan's action or character.
segments.s225-g90: که‌ای شیر دل مرد پرخاشجوی is the vocative/exclamatory address Homan utters. که‌ای (here rendered as an exclamatory particle + ای) introduces the direct address; شیر دل is a compound epithet 'lion‑hearted' (literal 'lion heart'), مرد here means 'man' in the sense of a valorous person, and پرخاشجوی is an adjective 'quarrelsome/aggressive' modifying مرد. The whole phrase is vocative: 'O lion‑hearted, quarrelsome man.'
segments.s225-g91: بدین زور و این برز و بالای تو groups a sequence of qualities that justify the claim: بدین is the prepositional combination (به + این) meaning 'by/with this', زور 'strength', و 'and', این برز 'this high/exalted stature' (برز = height/exaltation), و 'and', بالای تو 'your loftiness/high rank' (بالای + تو). Together they form a circumstantial phrase 'with this strength and this exalted rank of yours.'
segments.s225-g92: سر تخت ایران سزد جای تو states the result: سر تخت ایران 'the summit/head of Iran's throne' (سر = top/summit, تخت = throne, ایران = Iran). سزد is a present/poetic 3rd‑person impersonal verb 'it befits/it suits', and جای تو 'your place/seat'. The normal reading is 'the top of Iran's throne is fitting as your place.'
segments.s225-g93: نباشی جز از پهلوانی بزرگ is a wish/formulaic negative construction: نباشی is the negative 2nd‑person singular form (here optative/ hortative sense 'may you not be'), جز 'except', از introduces the source or quality, پهلوانی 'heroism/championship', and بزرگ 'great'. Together: 'may you be nothing except great heroism' → 'may you be none but a great champion.'
segments.s225-g94: وگر نامداری ز ایران سترگ offers an alternative: وگر 'and if / but if', نامداری 'renown/fame', ز (poetic زِ) 'from', ایران 'Iran', and سترگ is an archaic/poetic epithet here meaning 'mighty/illustrious' (used adjectivally of Iran). The clause means 'or, if famed, a celebrated name from mighty Iran.'
segments.s225-g95: بپرسیدی از گوهر و نام من is a conditional protasis using the past form بپرسیدی: here it functions as 'had you asked' (counterfactual past). از introduces the objects of inquiry, گوهر 'essence / jewel' (here likely 'origin/essence'), و 'and', نام من 'my name' (my identity or fame).
segments.s225-g96: به دل دیگر آمد ترا کام من is the apodosis: به دل دیگر آمد means 'would have come into another heart/come differently to the heart', ترا is the enclitic 2nd‑person object (ت + را) marking 'to you' (آمدت/آمد ترا), کام من 'my desire'. Grammatically, آمد takes the suffix ت (here shown separately) and the object/topic marker را; the clause reads 'my desire would have come to your heart otherwise.'
segments.s225-g129: که introduces a content clause ('that' / 'for') linking this line to what was just said. این شیردل رستم is a noun phrase 'this lion‑hearted Rostam'; the copula is omitted (typical in classical Persian poetry). زابلیست is a predicative adjective formed with -ist meaning 'is of Zabul' or 'Zabulian' (the suffix carries the copular force: Rostam 'is' of Zabul).
segments.s225-g130: برین لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'on/upon this army' — بر 'on/upon' + ین (an orthographic/poetic variant of این) giving 'this'. اکنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. بباید is the older/poetic present/obligative ('one must' / 'it is necessary'); it governs the following verb. گریست is the verb meaning 'to lament/weep' used here after بباید (classical sequence: بباید + verb = 'one must [verb]').
segments.s225-g131: که again introduces the clause. هرگز ('never') negates the verb. نتابند is from تابیدن/tافتن in the sense 'to endure/withstand' (here 'be able to stand against'); the verb is in a classical present/subjunctive/optative form expressing general inability. با او marks the adversary 'against him'. به جنگ 'in battle' is the location/field of contest.
segments.s225-g132: This is a pair of parallel prepositional phrases that give the fields where no one can contend with him: به خشکی پلنگ = 'on land (a) leopard' and به دریا نهنگ = 'in the sea (a) whale'. The nouns پلنگ and نهنگ function like appositive similes/metaphors; the copula is omitted (elliptical classical style).
segments.s225-g133: سخن گفت is a classical verbal phrase 'he spoke' (literally 'said words'); گفت here functions as the past action 'said/spoke'. و links to بشنید 'and he heard'. پاسخ بسی is the object of بشنید: پاسخ 'reply/answer' qualified by بسی 'many/ample' — 'many replies' or 'an ample reply'.
segments.s225-g134: همی is a classical particle often indicating continuity or emphasis ('then/again/continually'). یاد کرد literally 'made remembrance' but here = 'recounted' or 'mentioned'. از introduces the topic/subject matter ('about/concerning'); بد هر کسی = 'the faults/evils of every person' (بد as noun 'bad/evil', هر کسی 'every person'). Together: 'he continually recounted the faults of every man.'
segments.s225-g135: نخست‌ای is 'first, O...' — the adverb/ordering word نخست plus the vocative اَی (here written attached) addressing 'brother'. برادر is the vocative 'O brother'. مرا نام برد is 'he mentioned my name' (مرا = me + را = object marker; نام برد literally 'took/said the name' = 'named/mentioned me'). The copular/auxiliary elements are classical/elliptical but the sequence is a straightforward past narration.
segments.s225-g136: ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from/about' introducing the topic. کین سیاوش = 'the vengeance/grudge of Siyavash' (a genitive phrase). بسی is an adverb 'much/many' modifying the verb that follows. برشمرد is a past verb meaning 'he recounted/enumerated' — 'he recounted many things about Siyavash's vengeance.'
segments.s225-g17: جز از تو at the start means 'except (for) you' (جز = 'except', از = 'from/of'); کسی را is the indefinite 'anyone' marked by the object/topic marker را (here introducing the person about whom the speaker speaks); ز ایران سپاه is a poetic order meaning 'an army/host from Iran' (ز = 'from', سپاه = 'army'). The whole phrase sets up the exception and the domain ('anyone in Iran's army, except you').
segments.s225-g18: ندیدم is the past negative of دیدن (I did not see / I have not seen). که introduces a relative clause 'who/that', linking to 'کسی'. دارد is the present third-person singular of داشتن 'has/possesses'. دل رزمگاه is the object/predicate of داشتن: 'a heart (دل) for the battlefield (رزمگاه)' — i.e. 'who has the heart for battle.'
segments.s225-g19: مرا مهربانیست is an existential/experiencer construction: مرا (to me / for me) marks the experiencer and مهربانیست = مهربانی + ست ('is kindness' / 'there is kindness'), so the sense is 'I feel affection' or 'I have kindness toward'. بر introduces the target 'toward' and مرد جنگ is a compound 'man of war' = 'warrior'.
segments.s225-g20: بویژه ('especially') is an adverb emphasizing the upcoming relative clause. که introduces the clause 'who/that'. دارد (has) is the present of داشتن. نهاد here means 'innate nature / disposition' and پلنگ is a figurative epithet 'leopard/panther', so the clause says 'especially one who has a leopard-like disposition.'
segments.s225-g21: کنون‌گر بگویی is an archaic/poetic conditional phrasing: کنون‌گر = 'if now' (introductory conditional), and بگویی is the subjunctive/2nd-person form 'you say / you tell' (i.e. 'if you tell'). مرا here is the dative 'to me'. نام خویش = 'your name' where خوی + ش (خویش) means 'one's own / of one' so خوی (kin/lineage) + clitic ش = 'your'.
segments.s225-g22: This is a paratactic list introduced by the preposition بر (here functioning like 'about/pertaining to' or 'concerning') and joined by و 'and'. بوم = 'homeland', پیوند = 'bond / kinship / alliance', و آرام خویش = 'and your repose/place of rest' with خویش (خوی + ش) again 'one's own / his/her own' indicated by the clitic ش. The preposition and the conjunctions link these nouns as the items requested after 'tell me'.
segments.s225-g23: سپاسی برین کار is 'thanks for this deed' (سپاسی = thanks; برین = بر + این 'for this' in poetic spelling). بر من نهی uses نهی as a prohibitive/negative imperative 'do not (place/give) to me' — idiomatically 'do not thank me' or 'don't give me thanks for this deed.'
segments.s225-g24: کز is a classical connective meaning 'for that reason / because' (introducing the cause). اندیشه = 'thought' (thinking), گردد is the present/subjunctive/poetic form of شدن/گشتن 'becomes' (here 'becomes' or 'grows'), and دل من تهی = 'my heart empty' (تهی = empty). Together: 'because by thinking (of it) my heart becomes empty.'
segments.s225-g145: برو is the imperative 'go'. تا introduces purpose/result here ('so that/in order that'), linking to the following verb. ببینیش is ببینی + ـش (object pronoun 'him') meaning '(so that) you may see him'; نیزه به دست is a postposed phrase 'spear in hand' (به + دست = 'with/in the hand').
segments.s225-g146: تو گویی که is an idiomatic comment: تو + گویی (here functioning like 'it seems / one would say') + که 'that'. بر کوه دارد نشست is a verbal clause where دارد (auxiliary) + نشست (sit/sat) gives the sense 'is/has sat' or 'is sitting upon' — بر کوه = 'on the mountain'.
segments.s225-g147: This segment lists garments/items joined by و 'and'. ابا here is the garment 'aba' (a cloak) rather than the verb 'to refuse' (sense not offered in the list). جوشن = 'cuirass/armor'. ترگ = 'rag/shred'. ببر = 'tiger' (here 'tiger-skin' implied). بیان is part of the clothing list (a kind of mantle/covering in context) rather than 'expression' in the literal sense.
segments.s225-g148: به زیر اندرون = 'beneath/under the inner (garments)' with به indicating direction/locative ('into/under'). ژنده = 'ragged/tattered'. پیلی here describes something elephantine (an elephant-hide or elephant‑like material). ژیان in this context is part of the garment description and does not carry its usual senses 'life / way of life' given in the list, so its precise lexical sense isn't in the provided options.
segments.s225-g149: ببینی که = 'you will see that' (ببینی here is subjunctive/future-oriented 'you shall see' in narrative). من زین نجستم = 'I leapt/sprang from this saddle' (زین = 'this saddle'; نجستم = past 'I leapt'). دروغ is a parenthetical 'not a lie' or an assertion about truthfulness (in context it negates the following claim or emphasizes it's not false).
segments.s225-g150: همی گیرد is a present/habitual statement where همی (here 'indeed/continually') modifies گیرد ('takes/seizes'). آتش ز تیغش فروغ = 'the fire (or flame) takes radiance from his sword' — ز = 'from', تیغش = 'his sword', فروغ = 'radiance/brightness'. The clause describes the sword's flashing brilliance.
segments.s225-g151: ترا (ت + را here representing the object 'you') is the object 'you'. تا نبیند = 'until (he) sees' with نبیند as a subjunctive/present form meaning 'sees'. نجنبد = negative-present 'does not move/stir' (the negative prefix ن- plus جنبیدن). ز جای = 'from his place'. The word order places the object (ترا) before تا-clause for emphasis.
segments.s225-g152: ز بهر تو مانده‌ست = 'he has remained for your sake' (ز بهر = 'for the sake of', مانده‌ست = 'has remained'). زان سان به پای = 'so/thus at (your) feet' where زان سان = 'in that manner/so' and به پای = 'at the foot/at (your) feet' (به marks the locative 'at').
segments.s225-g49: وزان پس is a temporal/sequence phrase ('from that / after that'); بگویم is 1st person singular present/subjunctive 'I will say / I tell' (poetic narrative present). به marks the indirect object 'to' (to Kai Khosrow). این is a demonstrative picking up the following clause. Word order is typical Persian: temporal phrase + verb + prepositional object + demonstrative.
segments.s225-g50: بشویم is a subjunctive/passive-form of شدن 'become' here expressing a wished-for change ('may ... become' / 'let ... be'). دل و مغزش are the two coordinated internal organs ('heart and his brain'), with the -ش suffix on مغز marking possession. از introduces the cause/contents ('from / full of'), followed by the nouns درد و کین 'pain and hatred'.
segments.s225-g51: به تو introduces the indirect object 'to you.' برشمارم is 1st person singular present/subjunctive of شماردن 'to count / to enumerate' ('I will count / I count'). کنون is a temporal adverb 'now.' نامشان is a possessed noun 'their name(s) / their fame.' The clause order is prepositional object + verb + temporal + direct object.
segments.s225-g52: که introduces a wishing/optative clause here (rendered in English with 'may...'). مه is a poetic simile 'the moon' modifying نامشان ('their name'). باد is the optative particle 'may it be' (classical/poetic Persian). و links the two parallel wishes; کامشان 'their desire' is the second parallel noun. The construction is elliptic: 'that their name may be the moon and their desire (may be so)'.
segments.s225-g53: سر کین is a nominal phrase 'the head/source of hatred' (سر used metaphorically as 'source/chief'). ز marks origin 'from' and introduces the agent/place (گرسیوز). آمد is the past of آمدن 'came' and نخست 'first' is an adverb modifying آمد; the clause says that the source of enmity came first from Garsivaz.
segments.s225-g54: که begins a relative clause modifying the preceding noun (’which/who’). درد دل is a genitive-like collocation 'the pain of the heart' (emotional suffering). و joins رنج ایران 'the suffering of Iran'; بجست is past tense of جستن used here in the sense 'sought out / searched for' (classical usage).
segments.s225-g55: کسی را is a topicalized/definite object ('someone' with the object marker را). که introduces a relative clause 'whom/that.' دانی تو is 'you know' (present tense, second person). از تخم کور literally 'from the seed of the blind' — a figurative origin phrase ('descended from the blind stock'). Word order: topical object + relative marker + verb + subject + origin phrase.
segments.s225-g56: که again introduces a subordinate/relative clause. بر is a preposition 'upon/against/over'; خیره is an adjective 'astonished / staring' modifying an implied people; این آب 'this water' is the direct object; کردند is 3rd person plural past 'they made/did.' شور here means 'tumult/commotion' — so the clause says they made this water tumultuous before the astonished.
segments.s225-g73: وگر introduces a conditional (“if / but if”). جز بدین گونه is a negating/exceptive phrase: بدین گونه = 'in this way' (بـ- = the prepositional prefix *be/ba* + دین as part of the frozen form بدین), and جز = 'except / other than'. گویی here is a verbal form 'say / should say' (present/subjunctive/optative sense) that forms a verb phrase with the following کنَم; سخن is the object 'words / speech' placed after the verb phrase by poetic word order.
segments.s225-g74: کنم is the 1st-person verbal form 'I do / I would do' and completes the potential/conditional started by وگر ... گویی. تازه modifies پیکار and means 'fresh / renewed'; پیکار 'battle'. و links the coordinated second noun کین کهن (کین 'vengeance/hatred', کهن 'old/ancient'), so the clause predicates that the speaker would cause 'fresh battle and ancient vengeance.'
segments.s225-g75: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause ('for / who'). خوکرده is a past-participial/adjectival form meaning 'accustomed' or 'habituated' and with منم ('I am') expresses 'I am accustomed to ...'. جنگ توران is a noun phrase 'the war/battles of Turan' (genitival relation), placed before the copula منم.
segments.s225-g76: یکی here is an indefinite 'one / a certain' (i.e. 'a certain famous man'); نامداری is a nominal meaning 'renown / a notable (one)'. از ایران marks origin or affiliation 'from/of Iran'. منم is the emphatic copula 'I am' (repeated for force). The whole phrase states the speaker's identity as a famous man of Iran.
segments.s225-g77: بسی here (classical/poetic) means 'many / much' and quantifies سر. سر جدا کرده دارم is a compound verbal construction: جدا کرده (past participle 'having cut off / separated') plus دارم 'I have' gives a present-perfect-like meaning 'I have cut off'. ز تن is a prepositional phrase 'from the body/bodies' (ز = 'from').
segments.s225-g78: که begins a result/explanatory clause 'so that / and indeed'. جز ... نبودش is an idiom 'there was nothing except ...' (نبودش = 'there was not for it / it did not have', the clitic -ش refers back to the bodies/heads). کام means 'desire / appetite'; شیران here most naturally reads 'lions' (though poetically it can mean 'brave men'); کفن 'shroud'. The clause says their only shroud was the appetite of the lions.
segments.s225-g79: مرا آزمودی is a 2nd-person perfect 'you tested me' (مرا = 'me' with the object marker را; the provided split shows 'م' + 'را' but functionally it is the pronominal object). آزمودی is past/perfect of آزمودن 'to test / try'. بدین رزمگاه = 'in/at this battlefield' (بـ- + دین = 'to/at this', رزمگاه = 'battlefield'). The whole phrase addresses the addressee: 'you have tried me in this battlefield.'
segments.s225-g80: همینست is a fixed copular/expletive phrase 'this is so / that is exactly it'. رسم 'custom' and راه 'way/path' are the two coordinated nouns linked by و; repeating همینست before each noun is emphatic: 'this is the custom and this is the way.'
segments.s225-g25: بدو (a prepositional/clitic phrase) precedes the verb and means “to him/unto him,” so the sequence بدو گفت رستم literally reads “to him said Rostam” (Rostam said to him). گفت is the past tense verb “said.” که introduces the content clause (what was said). چندین سخن is a noun phrase “many/several words” serving as the topic of the reported speech.
segments.s225-g26: که begins the reported clause “that/which.” گفتی is the second-person past form “you said/spoke” (here used as part of the accusation), and و joins the following verb. افگندی is second-person past “you threw/hurled.” از مهر بن is a small genitive phrase where از “from” + مهر “affection/love” + بن “root/base” yields “from the root of affection” (a poetic way to say ‘out of/from your affection’ or ‘from the seat of love’).
segments.s225-g27: چرا is the interrogative “why.” تو is the explicit subject “you.” نگویی here functions as a negative/interrogative present/subjunctive sense (“do you not say / won't you tell”), addressing the addressee and introducing a request. م (the clitic for me) plus را (the definite-object marker) together mark the indirect object “to me” (مرا = “me” with object marker). نام خویش is “your own name,” with خویش expressed via the stem خوی + enclitic ش (‘your/his’) attached.
segments.s225-g28: A coordination of possessions: بر و کشور و بوم و آرام — a string of nouns meaning roughly “lands/realm and country and homeland and resting-place.” The final خویش + ش is the possessive enclitic that applies to the whole series, so these are “your” lands, country, homeland, and rest. The conjunction و links the items in a simple list; the possessive enclitic is only attached to the last noun (a common Persian construction).
segments.s225-g29: چرا introduces the question “why.” آمده‌ستی is the second-person perfect/past compound meaning “you have come” or idiomatically “you came/you have come.” به نزدیک من is a prepositional phrase “near me” (به + adjective/noun نزدیک + objectِِِِِِِِِِِِِِِ = “to/near me”).
segments.s225-g30: This is an adverbial list introduced by به “with/in”: نرمی “gentleness/softness,” چربی literally “fat/grease” but here used figuratively as slickness or flattery, and چندین سخن “many words” (repeated speeches). The items are linked by و and together describe the manner of his approach.
segments.s225-g31: اگر introduces a conditional “if.” آشتی is the noun “reconciliation” (the thing sought). جست is used here in the sense “seek” (classical/poetic past or perfect-stem form). خواهی همی is an archaic/fixed verbal cluster where خواهی supplies will/obligation and همی is an emphatic/imperfective particle; together they give the sense “you should/will (indeed)” or a hortative “do (try).”
segments.s225-g32: بکوشی is the jussive/subjunctive form “strive/endeavor (you).” که introduces the result or purpose clause “that/so that.” این کینه is “this grudge/hatred.” کاهی is the simile/metaphor “(like) straw,” i.e. insignificance — the speaker urges that the grudge be reduced to something as trivial as straw. همی at the end is emphatic/continuous, reinforcing the resultive wish.
segments.s225-g137: ز کار گذشته = a prepositional/topic phrase 'from/about past deeds'. بسی کرد یاد is a verbal phrase where بسی (an intensifier ‘much’) precedes the compound verb کرد یاد ('made remembrance' = 'remembered'). Word order is poetic: object/phrase + intensifier + light verb + noun of remembrance.
segments.s225-g138: ز introduces a new governed phrase 'regarding/among' (applies to both items). پیران و گردان ویسه‌نژاد lists two coordinated nouns 'the elders and the troop/host of the Viseh-lineage'; ویسه‌نژاد is a compound proper-name meaning 'of Viseh's stock.'
segments.s225-g139: This is a coordinated 'from...' phrase: ز بهرام وز تخم گودرزیان. The middle item 'وز' is actually the fusion of و + ز ('and from') in poetic spelling; it functions as a conjunction + preposition linking 'بهرام' and 'تخم گودرزیان' (the seed/descendants of the Gudarzis).
segments.s225-g140: ز هر کس که آمد = 'from whoever came' (هر کس که = 'whoever'), followed by بریشان زیان where بر (here 'upon/against') is combined with the 3pl enclitic ایشان 'ـیشان' yielding 'to/against them', and زیان ('harm') functions as the predicate: 'harm came upon them' (poetic word order places the noun at the end).
segments.s225-g141: بجز بر تو بر کس = 'except toward you, toward no one' (بجز marks the exception). ندیدمش = 'I did not see him' (the object pronoun -مش = 'him' enclitic to the verb), and مهر ('affection, love') is the thing not seen in him: literally 'I did not see in him affection for anyone but you.' The double prepositional phrase emphasizes the exception.
segments.s225-g142: فراوان سخن گفت = 'he spoke much/at length' where فراوان is an adverb modifying گفت. و نگشاد چهر = 'and did not open/show his face' — نگشاد is negative past (ن- + گشاد 'opened'), and چهر ('face, countenance') is the object; idiomatically 'did not show his countenance/express himself.'
segments.s225-g143: ازین لشکر = 'from this army' (از + این written here as از + ین). ترا را خواسته‌ست = 'he has asked/desired you' where ترا is the object ('you') realized as the enclitic ت + the object marker را; خواسته‌ست is the perfect/past participle with auxiliary 'has wanted/asked'. The doubled object marking (enclitic + را) is a poetic/colloquial emphasis.
segments.s225-g144: ندانم 'I do not know' introduces an indirect question: که بر دل چه آراسته‌ست = 'what he has arranged/settled upon his heart' (که = 'that/what', بر دل = 'on the heart/mind', آراسته‌ست = 'is adorned/has arranged' used idiomatically for 'has in mind/has prepared').
segments.s225-g81: ازین گونه is a frozen prepositional phrase meaning "in this manner/way": از (here 'from/in') + ین (an older/poetic spelling of 'این' = 'this') + گونه ('manner'). هرگز is an adverb of negation 'never'. نگفتم is past tense, 1st person singular with the negative prefix ن- plus the past stem گفتـ, so 'I did not say / I never spoke'. سخن is the noun 'speech/words' and here functions as the content object of نگفتم (poetic word order places the object after the verb).
segments.s225-g82: بجز marks exception 'except/aside from'. کین is the noun 'vengeance/hatred' and is the thing excepted. نجستم is past, 1st person singular 'I sought' (poetic form of جستن), so the clause means 'I sought (nothing) but vengeance'. ز سر تا به بن is a prepositional expression 'from head to root/base' (ز = 'from', سر = 'top/head', تا = 'as far as/until', به بن = 'to the root/base'); it intensifies the scope of seeking (i.e. from top to bottom).
segments.s225-g83: کنون places the time 'now'. هرچ is a relative/indefinite pronoun 'whatever/whichever'. گفتم is past 1st person 'I said', and ترا is the clitic form 'to you' (تو + را) marking the indirect object/recipient: 'whatever I said to you'. گوش دار is an imperatival idiom formed from the noun گوش ('ear/attention') plus دار from داشتن in an imperative sense 'have/hold', so together 'pay attention / listen'.
segments.s225-g84: سخنهای is the plural/collective of سخن 'words/speeches' ('the words'). خوب qualifies it 'good/fine'. اندر is an older/pre-classical preposition meaning 'in/within'. آغوش is 'bosom/embrace' and دار here repeats the verb sense 'hold/keep' (imperative), so the phrase literally means 'keep good words in your bosom' (i.e. cherish/accept them).
segments.s225-g85: چو is the conjunction 'when'. بشنید is past tense 'he heard' (poetic order places verb before name). هومان is the proper name 'Homan' (the person who heard). بترسید is past tense 'he was afraid' (b‑ + ترسید); سخت is an adverbial intensifier 'severely/strongly', modifying بترسید ('he was greatly afraid').
segments.s225-g86: بلرزید is past 'he trembled' (intransitive). بر سان is a simile: بر = 'on/upon' and سان = 'like/as', so 'as/on the likeness of'. برگ درخت is the image 'a leaf of a tree' — together: 'he trembled like a leaf on a tree'. Note the typical Persian simile order 'بر سان X' = 'like X'.
segments.s225-g87: کزان is a compound preposition 'from which/therefrom' (ک+ از + آن) functioning as 'from that'. گونه گفتار means 'that kind of speech/utterance' (گونه = 'manner/type', گفتار = 'speech'). رستم is the subject (Rostam). شنید is past 'he heard' or 'he perceived/learned' — together: 'Rostam heard/perceived from that kind of speech'.
segments.s225-g88: همه is 'all/entirely' and modifies کینه 'hatred/grudge'. از introduces the source 'from'; دوده here is best taken as 'soot/filth' (a moral metaphor), and خویش + ش is the reflexive possessive 'his own' (خوی = disposition/temper, ش = his). دید is past 'he saw/perceived'. The whole clause says he saw that all the hatred sprang from his own soot/disposition.
segments.s225-g153: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” بینیش here functions as a sight/meeting verb phrase (classical formation) — “when you see/meet him.” با او = “with/to him.” سخن نرم گوی is an imperative clause where سخن (“speech”) + نرم (“gentle”) form the object/description and گوی (poetic imperative of گفتن) is the verb at the end: “speak gently.” Word order is (conjunction) + (seeing) + (prepositional phrase) + (object) + (imperative verb).
segments.s225-g154: برهنه مکن is a negative command: مکن (negative imperative of کردن) = “do not,” with برهنه (“make bare/expose”) as its complement — “do not expose/bare.” The sentence then strings another negative command: تیغ و منمای روی = “(do not) (the) sword and (do not) show the face.” منمای is another negative imperative form meaning “do not show/reveal,” and روی (“face”) is its direct object. The two prohibitions are coordinated by و.
segments.s225-g155: بدو گفت پیران is inverted word order: بدو = “to him” (the clitic prepositional object), گفت (here treated as ‘said’) and پیران = “the elders.” The sequence means “the elders said to him.” که‌ای رزمساز is a vocative address: که‌ای here functions like ای (O), so رزمساز = “warrior” is being addressed.
segments.s225-g156: بترسم is a present/future‑oriented clause meaning “I fear” (ب‑ prefix + ترسم). که introduces the content clause “that …” روز بد آید فراز = “an ill/bad day will come upon (us/from on high).” آید is the present/subjunctive form “come” and فراز is an adverbial/locative sense “upon/from above,” giving the sense “come upon us.”
segments.s225-g157: گر is the conditional conjunction “if.” ایدونک is an adverbial/particle meaning roughly “perhaps” or “it may be.” این تیغ‌زن is the noun phrase “this swordsman.” رستمست is the archaic fused copula/identifier: رستم + ‑ست, here meaning “is Rostam” or “belongs to Rostam/is Rostamesque,” so the clause reads “if perhaps this swordsman is Rostam.”
segments.s225-g158: بدین دشت is a locative phrase “in/at this plain” (ب- = prepositional prefix ‘in/to’ + دین/این orthography yielding ‘this’ in the poem). ما را uses ما + the object/topic marker را meaning “for us” or marking the affected party. گه is an adverb “at times/sometimes,” and ماتمست (ماتم + است) is a predicate “there will be mourning” (classical fusion of copula). Together: “in this plain there will sometimes be mourning for us.”
segments.s225-g159: بر آتش بسوزد is a verbal clause with an impersonal/inauspicious sense: بر آتش = “on/into fire” and بسوزد (from سوزاندن/سوختن, present/subjunctive form) = “will burn” or “may burn.” بر و بوم ما is the compound “land and dwelling (our homeland),” with بر repeated poetically; the phrase functions as the subject/affected domain: “our land and home will burn.” Word order places the verb near the front for poetic emphasis.
segments.s225-g160: ندانم is the first‑person present “I do not know.” چه introduces the interrogative/object clause “what.” کرد is the past/infinitival sense of “do/make” here meaning “will do” in the projected sense. اختر is “star” used idiomatically for fate/omening, and شوم is the first‑person subjunctive “I may become” or “(that) befall us” — together the phrase expresses uncertainty about what the star/fate will do to them. ما at the end reiterates the affected party “to us.”
segments.s225-g177: شوم بنگرم is a volitional/hortative opening: شوم (lit. “may I become”) functions as a poetic volitive (‘let me’ / ‘I will’) combined with بنگرم, the present-subjunctive/optative form of نگریستن (“to look”), so together they mean “let me look” or “I will look.” تا introduces purpose (‘so that / in order that’), چه is an interrogative ‘what’, خواهد is third-person modal/future (‘he will / he wants’), and همی is the archaic continuous/aspectual particle adding emphasis (‘still/indeed’).
segments.s225-g178: که introduces a subordinate (purpose/result) clause: ‘that / so that’. از is the preposition ‘from/of’, غم means ‘sorrow’, روانم is روان + possessive -م ‘my soul/spirit’, and بکاهد is the third-person present/subjunctive of کاستن used optatively ‘may (he) lessen’. همی again is the archaic aspectual particle marking continuous/emphatic sense. The clause means ‘that he may relieve my spirit of sorrow.’
segments.s225-g179: بدو گفت خاقان is normal Classical Persian word order where the indirect object/goal (بدو ‘to him’) precedes the verb گفت (here past ‘said’) and the subject خاقان follows; literally ‘to him said the khaghan.’ برو is the imperative ‘go’, پیش ‘before / in the presence of’, and اوی is the classical form for ‘him/her’ (object).
segments.s225-g180: چنانچون ‘as / just as’ begins a simile/comparative; بباید is an impersonal/modal archaic form ‘it is fitting / one ought to’; سخن نرم گوی is an imperative construction where سخن (‘speech’) is modified by نرم (‘gentle’) and گوی is the verb ‘say / speak’ (here imperative/command ‘speak’). Word order places the adjective before the noun it qualifies and the verb at the end, as is common in poetry.
segments.s225-g181: اگر is the conditional ‘if’. آشتی is a noun ‘reconciliation / truce’. خواهد here means ‘wishes / desires’ (present/modal) and is attached to the noun to mean ‘if he wants reconciliation’. و is the simple conjunction ‘and’. دستگاه is used as ‘arrangement / settlement / terms’ (an abstract ‘system/settlement’ here).
segments.s225-g182: چه باید is a rhetorical question ‘what should (be done)’. بر + ین (here orthographic split of این) forms برین ‘on this’; دشت is ‘plain’, and رنج سپاه is a noun phrase ‘the hardships/sufferings of the army’ (poetic omission of ezāfe is common). The whole asks what must be done regarding the army’s toil on this plain.
segments.s225-g183: بسی here is a poetic quantifier ‘much / many’; هدیه is ‘gift(s)’. بپذیر is the imperative ‘accept’. و پس باز گرد is a sequence of adverbs/verbs: و ‘and’, پس ‘then/afterwards’, باز گرد (بازگرد) ‘return’ — باز (again) + گرد (imperative of گشتن/گردیدن ‘go/turn’), so ‘then return’. The verb imperatives come at the ends of these clauses.
segments.s225-g184: سزد‌گر is a conditional concessive: سزد (‘it befits’) + گر (‘if’), i.e. ‘if it is fitting’. نجوییم is the first-person-plural present-subjunctive/optative of جستن/جوییدن with negative prefix ن- here meaning ‘let us not seek / let us not pursue’ (poetic form). چندین is a quantifier ‘many / several’ and نبرد ‘battles’. The clause therefore offers a conditional recommendation: ‘if it be fitting, let us not pursue many battles.’
segments.s225-g169: گر is the conditional particle 'if'. افراسیاب is the proper name (subject). آید is the classical present/subjunctive form of آمد (here: 'should come' / 'comes'); اکنون is the temporal adverb 'now' placed after the verb (classical word order is flexible). چو + آب begins a simile ('like/as water') that is completed by the next segment.
segments.s225-g170: نبینند is the negative present/optative 3rd-person plural form of دیدَن — after a conditional it functions like 'they would not see' or 'they will see not'. جز is the exception particle 'except'. سهم او را is 'his portion' with را marking and emphasizing the object (classical topical/object marking). به خواب means 'in a dream' / 'in sleep' (چو آب in the previous segment sets up a poetic simile referring to the manner/content of what is seen).
segments.s225-g171: ازو is the classical form of 'از او' ('from him' / 'out of him'). دیو is 'demon(s)' (subject of the verb). سیر here is poetic and modifies the idea of abundance/fulness (in classical usage it can imply 'full/satiate' or 'many'); it intensifies the following verb. اید is an archaic/poetic subjunctive/optative form (may he/they come / would come) — used here to indicate potential or habitual coming. اندر نبرد is a locative phrase 'into the battle' (اندر = in/into, نبرد = battle).
segments.s225-g172: چه ... چه ... is a correlative construction meaning 'whether ... or ...'. یک مرد is 'one man' (a single warrior). با او literally 'with him' (here contextual: ‘beside/with or against him’ — classical Persian allows such ambiguity; the sense is 'regardless of whether a single man stands with him'). چه یک دشت مرد is 'or one (whole) plain of men' — دشت 'plain' here poetically indicates a great number (a host).
segments.s225-g173: به زابلستان is a locative/locution placing the scene in Zabulistan ('in/at Zabulistan'); classical Persian uses به for locative sense. چند here means 'several' / 'numerous'. پرمایه is an adjective meaning 'substantial', 'of substance' or 'prosperous/valuable' describing those people. بود is the past tense of بودن 'there were' (simple past / existential).
segments.s225-g174: سیاوش را uses را to topicalize/mark Sīyāvash as the affected party; آن زمان is the temporal phrase 'at that time'. دایه 'nurse' is the predicate noun and بود is the copula 'was' — the construction 'X را Y بود' functions idiomatically as 'X had Y' or 'X was possessed of Y' in classical prose/poetry: 'Siyavash had a nurse at that time.'
segments.s225-g175: پدروار is an adjective/adverb 'fatherly' or 'in a father-like manner'. با + درد 'with pain/sorrow' indicates the accompanying feeling. جنگ آورد is a verbal phrase where آورد is the past stem of آوردن 'to bring/cause'; together 'brought/caused war' — poetically: 'he caused war, accompanied by fatherly sorrow.' Note classical inversion: manner and afflictions are fronted before the verb.
segments.s225-g176: جهان is the noun 'the world' (here used metonymically for life/the realm). بر is the preposition 'upon/against'. جهاندار 'world-ruler' / 'sovereign' is the affected party. تنگ آورد is a compound where تنگ (narrow/tight) with آوردن means 'made narrow' or 'made life constricted for' — i.e. 'he made the world tight for the sovereign' (he caused hardship for the king).
segments.s225-g113: "ز خون سیاوش جگرخسته اوست" is built with a causal prepositional phrase first: ز + noun gives the cause ('because of / from'). جگرخسته is a compound adjective ('heart‑weary, grief‑stricken') that appears before the copula. اوست is the third‑person copula ('he is'), the clitic subject pronoun (او) attached to ست; word order here is cause → predicate → copula.
segments.s225-g114: This clause parallels the first: ز ترکان marks the sphere/target ('with regard to / toward the Turks'), کنون is the temporal adverb 'now', and the two predicate adjectives راد و آهسته ('generous and soft/gentle') precede the copula اوست ('he is'). The coordinating و links the two adjectives; the whole is a simple nominal predicate with a prefixed prepositional phrase for direction/relationship.
segments.s225-g115: سوی (toward) precedes the destination; من is the object ('me'). فرستش is فرست + ـش, where ـش is the object clitic 'him' — so this is an instruction 'send him (فرست + ـش)'. هم‌اکنون is the adverb 'right now'; دمان (literally 'hem/skirt') is used poetically as 'to my presence' (the poetical/metaphorical noun placed after the verb phrase).
segments.s225-g116: ببینیم is a first‑person plural optative/subjunctive 'let us see'. تا introduces the subordinate/result clause ('so that / in order that' or more loosely 'let us see what...'). بر introduces the target of change ('to/into what'); چه is the interrogative 'what'; گردد is a third‑person singular present subjunctive of گشت/گردیدن 'become, turn into'; زمان is the noun 'time' and is the semantic subject of گردد. Together: 'let us see into what the time will turn.'
segments.s225-g117: بدو (bedo) is the prepositional phrase 'to him / unto him' placed before the verb in a typical Classical Persian order ('to him said Homan'). گفت is the past tense 'said' (here listed as the verb) with هومان the subject (proper name); the whole phrase means 'Homan said to him'. که‌ای here functions as an archaic/exclamatory vocative construction (que + ey) addressing someone: 'O ...' and is followed by the epithet سرفراز ('noble/exalted'), so the phrase is an addressed greeting 'O noble one'.
segments.s225-g118: بدیدار is a verbal-noun/infinitive 'to visit / to see' (here 'to visit'); پیرانت is 'pīrān' + second-person suffix -ت, 'your elders'; آمد is the past tense 'came'; نیاز is a noun 'petition / entreaty / supplication'. The clause follows Classical Persian order where the verbal idea 'آمد' appears after the noun phrase: literally 'to-visit your-elders came a-petition' → 'a petition has come to visit your elders.'
segments.s225-g119: چه is the interrogative 'what' introducing the question. دانی is the second-person singular present of دانستن 'you know'. تو is the emphatic/explicit subject pronoun 'you'. پیران و کلباد را is the direct object (pīrān 'elders' and Kalbad, a proper name) marked by the object marker را after the coordinated noun phrase; the clause asks 'What do you know about the elders and Kalbad?'
segments.s225-g120: گروی here is an archaic/Poetic-form verb form (present/2nd-person shape of گرویدن in Classical Persian) meaning roughly 'you take/accept/join' or more loosely 'concerning/relating to' in this rhetorical context; the speaker lists further objects. زره را و پولاد را are coordinated noun phrases 'the armor' and 'the steel', each with the object marker را repeated for emphasis (a common Classical Persian pattern). Together the clause stresses 'and what do you know about the armor and about the steel?' The exact nuance of گروی is literary/archaising rather than a straightforward modern usage.
segments.s225-g193: نه is a negation at the start. اندر هوا باشد is a literary phrasing meaning 'be in the air' (اندر = 'in', هوا = 'air'); باشد here is the optative/subjunctive/literary 'be' (used to state existence/location in high style). او را is the object/topic clitic ('him' with the object marker را), placed before the verb as in older/poetic word order. نبرد here is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning 'is carried/taken' (classical usage of نبرد < بردن), so the whole line says he is not carried into the air / battle will not take him there.
segments.s225-g194: دلت is دل + second-person enclitic -ت, so 'your heart'; را marks it as the (emphatic/affected) topic. چه is a rhetorical interrogative 'what/why' here. سوزی is a noun meaning 'burning/ache' (emotional pain); به + تیمار و درد = 'with regard to care and pain' (به = 'in/with/regarding', تیمار = 'care/solicitude', و = 'and', درد = 'pain/affliction'). The clause asks why your heart is burning with anxiety and sorrow.
segments.s225-g195: چنان دان is an imperative/hortative 'consider it so' or 'take it thus' (چنان = 'thus/so', دان = 'consider/know' used as an instruction). که‌گر is the fused conditional 'که گر' = 'if'. سنگ و آهن are the conjuncts 'stone and iron'. خورد is the classical 3rd-person form of خوردن used here in the sense 'befall/strike/hit' (literary use), so 'if stone and iron strike (it)'.
segments.s225-g196: همان = 'that same/just that'; تیر و ژوپین = 'arrow and gambeson' (weapon and armor). برو بگذرد is a two-part verbal sequence: برو (an intensifying/old-fashioned verb form 'go') + بگذرد (بگذرد = 'pass through/go past' in literary present/future); together they mean 'will go/pass right through'.
segments.s225-g197: به هر مرد = 'for/to each man'; ازیشان (از + ایشان) = 'of/from them' (i.e. 'each of their men'). ز ما = 'from us' or 'of ours' (poetic z = از). سیصدست is a fused predicate 'سیصد است' meaning 'it is three hundred' (here: 'there are three hundred [of ours]'). The line expresses a numerical superiority: for each enemy man there are 300 of ours.
segments.s225-g198: درین = در + این (درین = 'in this') + رزمگه = 'battlefield/arena'. غم کشیدن is a verbal phrase 'to suffer/undergo sorrow' (کشیدن = 'to suffer/endure' in this sense). بدست here functions adverbially 'by/through' or idiomatically 'is what one must do'—so the clause says that in this battlefield one must endure sorrow (i.e. one must suffer/undergo hardship).
segments.s225-g199: همین = 'this very/just this'; زابلی = 'Zābeli' (a nisba, 'from Zabul'); نامبردار = 'named/renowned' (an epithet); مرد = 'man/hero'. This is a nominal phrase identifying the person.
segments.s225-g200: ز پیلی = 'than the elephant' (ز = poetic 'from/of' used comparatively, پیلی = 'elephantine/elephant'); فزون نیست = 'is not more/exceeds not' (فزون = 'greater/more abundant', نیست = 'is not'); گاه نبرد = 'time/occasion of battle' (گاه = 'time/occasion', نبرد = 'battle' as a noun). Together: 'he is not greater than an elephant at the time of battle' (i.e. he is not superior to an elephant in battle).
segments.s225-g105: This is a nominal clause: همه هرچ (’all/whatever’) + گفتی (here used as ‘you said’) followed by the locative phrase بدین رزمگاه (‘in/at this battlefield’). Word order is typical Persian: quantifier + relative/pronoun (هرچ) + reported-verb + locative. Note that بدین is the preposition + demonstrative ('به + این') fused; the segment functions as the direct object/content being reported.
segments.s225-g106: یکایک is an adverb ‘one by one’ modifying the verb بگویم. بگویم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive (classical/polite future sense: ‘I will tell’/‘I shall tell’) formed with the b‑ prefix + present stem. به پیش سپاه is a prepositional phrase ‘in the presence of / before the army’ (به + پیش + سپاه) that marks the audience/location.
segments.s225-g107: همان پیش is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘there/just there, in that very presence’ (همان intensifies پیش ‘before/in front of’). منشور و خاقان چین are two coordinated nouns (‘the minister/inscribed-tablet?’ and ‘the Khaghan of China’); و links them. This phrase sets the scene/people present.
segments.s225-g108: بزرگان و گردان توران زمین is a coordinated noun phrase: بزرگان (‘the great ones/elders’) and گردان (‘the host/troop’) qualified by توران زمین (‘the land of Turan’, i.e. Turanian). The second noun phrase is postmodified by a genitive-like compound naming the country.
segments.s225-g109: بدو گفت رستم is a compact clause: بدو (‘to him’) is the prepositional pronoun, گفت is the past tense verb ‘said’, and رستم is the subject (Rostam). که introduces the reported content. نامم مجوی is a negative imperative/jussive: نامم (‘my name’) + مجوی (‘do not seek/ask’). The object pronoun and negative form are classical/poetic but follow normal Persian ordering (object before verb in the subordinate clause).
segments.s225-g110: ز من (‘from me’/‘on my account’) marks source/possession. هرچ دیدی is a free relative ‘whatever you saw’ with دیدی as the past (2nd person/relative) ‘you saw’. بدیشان بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive instruction: بدیشان (‘to them’) + بگوی (2nd-person present/subjunctive/imperative form ‘tell’); together ‘tell them’. The sequence gives the content Rostam commands to be reported.
segments.s225-g111: ز پیران (‘from/because of the elders’) is a causal prepositional phrase. مرا را (here split in the tokenization) yields the object focus: مرا (me) + را (definite object marker) — together marking the experiencer of the emotion. دل بسوزد همی uses بسوزد (classical present/jussive form of سوزاندن/سوختن) meaning ‘burns/aches’ and همی is the frequentative/adverbial particle marking continuous or habitual action (‘constantly/ever’). The clause says the speaker’s heart continually burns because of the elders.
segments.s225-g112: ز مهرش (‘from his love’) gives the cause. روان is the subject here meaning ‘spirit/soul’, preceding the verb as is common in Persian. برفروزد is a present/classical form meaning ‘sets alight/kindles’ or ‘illuminates’, and همی again marks continuous aspect. Together the clause: ‘from his love his soul is always kindled.’
segments.s225-g33: نگه کن is an imperative: نگه (look, behold) + کن (do) = 'look!' or 'consider!'. The following که begins a content/relative clause: خون سیاوش که ریخت = 'Siavash's blood that was shed' (خون = 'blood', سیاوش the name, که + ریخت forms a subordinate clause 'that/when ... was poured/was shed'). Note the older/poetic phrasing omits a modern auxiliary; ریخت here means 'was shed/poured'.
segments.s225-g34: چنین آتش کین is a nominal phrase 'such a fire of hatred' (چنین = 'such', آتش کین = 'fire of vengeance/hatred'). به ما بر ... بیخت is a compact poetic clause: بر + verb بیخت = 'befell/upon us', so the sequence means 'fell upon us' or 'came upon us and made us miserable' (بیخت here = 'wretched/miserable' as a predicate). که introduces the result/relative sense ('so that/that'), giving the effect of the preceding event.
segments.s225-g35: همان is a demonstrative 'that/same'. خون پرمایه گودرزیان = 'the substantial/strong blood of the Gudarzis': پرمایه is an adjective after خون, and گودرزیان (the Gudarzis) follows to indicate possession/association in this poetic order (no ezâfe written explicitly).
segments.s225-g36: که introduces a relative clause 'which ...'. بفزود is the classical/poetic form of 'افزود' with a b- prefix; here it means 'added' or 'caused to increase' (past/narrative sense inside the relative clause). چندین زیان بر زیان is the idiom 'many a loss, loss upon loss' — چندین modifies the first زیان and بر introduces the repeated زیان for emphasis ('upon loss').
segments.s225-g37: کجا here is rhetorical ('ever' / 'where, indeed?') forming a question about the possibility of elders being against Siavash. با + name expresses being 'against/with' someone; بدند is the past plural of 'بودن' ('they were') and here means 'were (hostile/against)'. The line reads as a rhetorical denial of such hostility.
segments.s225-g38: نجستند is negative past formed with the prefix ن + جستند = 'they did not seek' (جستند = 'they sought'); پیکار is the object 'battle'. و خامش بدند = 'and they remained silent' (خامش as predicate 'silent' + بدند 'they were'). Word order places the object before the verb in the poetic style.
segments.s225-g39: This compact noun group is poetic/devocalized: گنهکار = 'the guilty one'; خون سر بیگناه should be read as خونِ سرِ بیگناه = 'the blood on the head of the innocent' (سرِ بیگناه = 'the innocent's head'). Classical Persian poetry often omits written ezâfe links, so the sequence gives 'the guilty man's [act of] blood on the innocent's head' (i.e. the innocent's blood).
segments.s225-g40: نگر is the imperative 'look!' or 'consider!'. تا که + یابی is a purpose/result clause 'until/so that you may find' — یابی here is a subjunctive/optative form 'you may find/you find'. ز توران سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'an army from Turan' (ز is the poetic 'from').
segments.s225-g9: به یزدان is an oath formula: به + یزدان = “by God.” که here introduces the reason/explanation (“for/that”) that follows. بیزارم is an intransitive present-stative with the 1st-person clitic -م = “I am averse/disgusted”; از marks the object of that aversion (تاج و گاه = “crown and court/seat”). Word order is typical older/poetic Persian: the predicate (بیزارم) follows the introductory clause.
segments.s225-g10: که here continues/resumes the explanatory clause (renderable “for/that” or “that”); چون = “like/as.” تو is the 2nd-person pronoun. ندیدم is a past/perfective negative first-person form (ن‑ + دیدم) meaning “I have not seen.” یکی رزم‌خواه is the direct object: یکی (= “one / a single / someone”) + رزم‌خواه (“warrior, battle-seeker”), placed after the verb for emphasis as is common in epic style.
segments.s225-g11: ز تو is a poetic prepositional phrase “from/than you.” بگذرد is a 3rd-person singular verbal form (present/future or gnomic present) meaning “passes / passes by / exceeds” depending on context. زین سپاه بزرگ groups as a prepositional/nominal phrase: زین (an archaic/dialectal demonstrative/marker here) + سپاه بزرگ = “this great host/army.” The verb precedes the noun phrase, a not-uncommon word order in older Persian poetry.
segments.s225-g12: نبینم is a negative present/subjunctive first-person form meaning “I do not / I shall not see.” همی is an older adverb meaning “ever/still/continually” that gives the clause an emphatic, habitual flavor. نامداری is an abstract noun “renown/fame,” and سترگ here functions adjectivally to qualify it (in classical Persian usage = “great/lofty”); the verb-fronted order places the speaker’s seeing (نبینم) before the thing seen for emphasis.
segments.s225-g13: دلیری is a noun “bravery/valour.” که introduces a relative clause (“which/that”). چندین is an adverb meaning “many times / often.” بجوید is a 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of جویدن/جست‌و «to seek/desire,» here “seeks.” نبرد is the direct object “battle.” Word order places the relative clause after the head noun, as is standard.
segments.s225-g14: برآرد is a 3rd-person singular verb meaning “brings forth / produces.” همی gives it an ongoing/gnomic sense (“ever/continually”). از introduces the source phrase (از دل شیر = “from the lion’s heart”), where دل is “heart” and شیر is “lion.” گرد appears as the final noun/qualifier; here it functions idiomatically with the preceding phrase (often rendered “valour/ardour” or an emphatic quality) and is linked as the thing produced by the verb.
segments.s225-g15: ز marks origin/aboutness (“from/of”). شهر و نژاد = “city and lineage” (two sources of identity). The second ز + آرام خوی(ش) = “and from his calm/gentle disposition”: خوی = “disposition/character” and ش is the clitic pronoun (= his). The sequence lists sources/attributes in coordinate form; word order is a simple enumerative coordination.
segments.s225-g16: سخن‌گوی is a predicate/noun meaning “speaker” or “one who speaks / eloquent one.” The conjunction و links the next coordinating phrase. از introduces the next domain (“of/from”), تخمه و نام خویش = “his offspring/seed and his name/fame” (تخمه = offspring, نام = name/reputation) with the possessive clitic ش (here written as خویش split previously) referring back to the person. The segment is a coordinated list of the things of which he speaks or that are his attributes.
segments.s225-g121: 'بدو' is a prepositional/indirect-object phrase meaning 'to him'; it precedes the verb 'گفت' (here used as 'said'). 'چندین' functions adverbially as 'many times' or 'several times'. The phrase 'چه پیچی سخن' is an exclamatory NP: 'چه' marks exclamation ('what/how'), 'پیچی' is a noun meaning a 'twist' or 'cunning' (here 'cunning/contrivance'), and 'سخن' is the object 'words/speech' — together 'what crafty words'.
segments.s225-g122: This is an imperative negative clause. 'سر آب را' is a definite NP ('the head/top of the water') with object marker 'را'. 'سوی بالا' is a directional phrase 'toward/upward'. 'مکن' is the negative imperative 'do not (do)'. Word order places the object and adverbial before the negative imperative verb, typical in classical Persian.
segments.s225-g123: 'نبینی' is a present-tense/negative form built on 'دیدن' and here functions as 'you do not see' or 'do you not see'. The conjunction 'که' introduces a content clause. 'پیکار' is the head noun 'battle' and 'چندین سپاه' is a postposed genitive-like phrase 'of many armies' specifying the battle.
segments.s225-g124: This clause reports consequence/source. The item glossed 'بدویست' is an irregular/archaic form here (no provided sense fits exactly) and appears as a fused/archaic element; its precise lexical sense is uncertain in the provided list. 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from' indicating source; 'آمد' is simple past 'came/has come'; 'این رزمگاه' is the subject/NP 'this battlefield'. The clause thus says that 'from that (battle) this battlefield has come'.
segments.s225-g125: 'بشد' is the past/perfective of 'شدن' meaning 'became/it became'; here it predicates of هومان: 'Homan became ...'. 'تیز' is a predicative adjective 'swift/quick'. 'هم' emphasizes 'also/together'; 'اندر زمان' is a temporal phrase 'in the moment/at that time'. The order is verb + predicative adjective + subject + temporal adjunct, typical of epic diction.
segments.s225-g126: 'شده' is a perfective/passive participial form meaning 'having become' or 'having been done' and here forms an adverbial clause ('having become thus'). 'گونه' is used adverbially as 'in such a manner'. 'از روی' is a prepositional combination 'from/off the surface' and modifies the following motion. 'و آمد دمان' is the main clause 'and came to the ḍamān/foothill' with 'آمد' past 'came' and 'دمان' the place reached. Overall this segment describes the manner of movement and the arrival.
segments.s225-g127: 'به پیران' is an indirect-object prepositional phrase 'to the elders'. 'چنین گفت' is a reporting formula 'thus he said' (here 'گفت' is the speech verb—its provided senses list did not include the verbal 'to say'). 'کای' is a vocative particle 'O', and 'نیک‌بخت' is the vocative noun 'fortunate one'.
segments.s225-g128: 'بد افتاد' is a compound meaning 'an evil/ill befell' (افتاد = 'befell; happened'); the adjective 'بد' qualifies the misfortune. 'ما را' is the patient 'us' with object marker 'را' (we are those affected). 'ازین کار سخت' is an ablative/explanatory phrase 'from this hard affair' or 'because of this difficult undertaking' giving the cause. Word order places verb before the affected object and the causal phrase at the end.
segments.s225-g161: بشد is a past/perfective verbal form meaning 'became/appeared/arrived' (here: 'came/appeared before'). پیش + NP means 'in the presence of / before (the ruler)'; the phrase follows Persian head-final order where the verb precedes the prepositional phrase for poetic effect. پر از is the fixed adjectival construction 'full of', so پر از آب چشم literally 'eye(s) full of water' = 'tearful eyes'.
segments.s225-g162: This is a verbless nominal/ adjectival description in which two possessive/emotive organs are given: جگر خسته 'liver/seat of feeling weary' and دل پر از درد و خشم 'heart full of pain and anger'. Persian allows stacked noun+adjective phrases joined by و; پر از uses از to mark the material/content causing the fullness.
segments.s225-g163: بدو گفت is a high-register/poetic ordering meaning 'he said to him' where بدو is an indirect-object/adverbial 'to him' placed before گفت. کای is the vocative particle 'O', addressing شاه. تندی مکن is a negative imperative: تندی 'haste/fury' + مکن 'do not (do)' = 'do not be hasty'.
segments.s225-g164: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause here ('for/because'). اکنون 'now' temporalizes the statement; دگرگونه گشت is a compound where گشت (past of گشتن) means 'became/turned' and dگرگونه is the predicate adjective 'different/otherwise'; این سخن 'this speech/matter' is the theme — word order is shifted for emphasis in poetry: 'for now this speech has become different.'
segments.s225-g165: چو is the subordinating particle 'when'. کاموس گو را is a topical/definite phrase: کاموس‑گو (a compound/name) followed by را marking it as a definite/topic; سرآمد زمان = 'the end (سرآمد) of the time (زمان)' — the noun سرآمد takes زمان as its complement. The whole phrase = 'when the time of Kamūs‑go had run out.'
segments.s225-g166: همانگاه is an adverb 'immediately/at once'. برد here is a past tense of بردن 'to take' used in the sense 'seized/took' (poetic inversion follows): the normal prose order would be 'این گمان دل من را برد' but poetry places برد first, then the object; این دل من گمان = 'this suspicion/thought took my heart' (i.e. 'this suspicion took hold of my heart').
segments.s225-g167: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that'. این باره is 'this occasion/instance' (باره = occasion). آهنین is an adjectival modifier 'iron/steely' and رستمست is a contracted copula construction (= رستم است or 'is Rostam's / is like Rostam'); in context it means 'this occasion belongs to/should be handled by iron‑Rostam' or 'Rostam is iron (on this occasion)'. Word order is compacted for verse.
segments.s225-g168: که again links/explains. خام modifies کمندش (the possessive -ش = 'his/its'): خام کمندش = 'his crude/raw lasso'. خم اندر خم is a fixed poetic phrase 'a curve within a curve / bend upon bend' and خمست is the copula 'is' compressed into the poetic form; the line therefore asserts that his lasso is coiled/curved upon curve (i.e. complex/powerful).
segments.s225-g185: وگر is the conditional 'if' (archaic/poetic but straightforward). زیر چرم پلنگ is a prepositional/locative phrase 'beneath the leopard's hide/skin' with چرم 'hide/skin' following its modifier پلنگ 'leopard'. اندرست is an archaic compound copula (اندر + است) meaning 'is in/within' and completes the clause.
segments.s225-g186: همانا is an emphatic adverb 'truly/verily'. که introduces the content clause explaining the previous condition. رایش here means 'his disposition/opinion/temper'. به جنگ اندرست uses به + noun as a directional/state phrase 'in/for war', and اندرست again is the archaic 'is in' copula, so the clause says his nature is turned toward war.
segments.s225-g187: همه یکسره نیز is an adverbial cluster meaning 'all together / entirely / also' (emphatic subject adverbial). جنگ آوریم uses آوریم (1st person plural) as the main verb 'we bring/bring about' — here idiomatically 'we wage/undertake war'; in epic style آوریم expresses determination or a simple present action by 'we'.
segments.s225-g188: برو دشت functions as a directional phrase 'to the plain' (برو as motion 'go' used with دشت 'plain'); the clause then continues with پیکار تنگ آوریم where تنگ modifies پیکار ('close, narrow combat') and آوریم is the same 1st plural verb 'we bring/engage in', so the whole unit means 'we go to the plain and bring the battle to close quarters.'
segments.s225-g189: همه is the collective 'all/everyone'. پشت را is the direct object (را marks پشت 'back') and سوی یزدان کنیم literally means 'turn (it) toward God' — کنیم is 1st plural subjunctive/hortative 'let us do' or 'we should do', so the phrase is a hortatory command: 'let us all turn our backs toward God' (i.e. entrust ourselves to God / orient ourselves to God).
segments.s225-g190: به نیروی او is an instrumental phrase 'by his power' where به functions as 'by/with' (instrumental). رزم شیران کنیم uses رزم 'battle' with شیران (literally 'lions' but here a poetic epithet for brave men/warriors) and کنیم again as the 1st plural hortative/optative 'let us / we will do' — 'by His strength we will contend/fight like lions.'
segments.s225-g191: هم here is emphatic 'also/indeed'. او را تن is the object 'his body' (را marks it). از آهن و روی نیست is a negated existential/copular construction 'is not of iron nor (of) metal/face' — نیست is the negative copula. The clause denies any metallic or iron composition of his body/face.
segments.s225-g192: جز introduces an exception 'except/from only'. جز از خون ... نیست states what he actually consists of: 'except of blood and flesh and hair there is not' — i.e. he is nothing but blood, flesh and hair. Note وز is a poetic/conjunctive form (classical 'و' + 'ز') linking 'خون' with the following nouns; it functions as a conjunction in context (see senses).
segments.s225-g41: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing a noun phrase; مردان و اسپان is a coordinated noun phrase 'men and horses', and آراسته is a past‑participle/adjectival predicate 'adorned/arrayed' placed after the nouns (a typical epic word order where the adjective follows and qualifies the whole phrase).
segments.s225-g42: کز introduces a relative/relative‑like clause 'whom/that', ایران is the source, بیاورد is a 3rd‑person narrative form 'he brought' (classical/poetic present/past sense), and با خواسته here means 'at (his) wish / by his request' (با + noun indicating manner).
segments.s225-g43: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when' (here 'when/if'); یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/all at once' modifying the verb; سوی ما is a directional prepositional phrase 'toward us'; فرستید is the classical narrative/past form 'he sent/sends' or subjunctive in epic diction; باز 'again' emphasizes return or repetition.
segments.s225-g44: من is the subject 'I'; از جنگ ترکان is an oblique phrase 'from the war with the Turks' (از + noun + genitive/adjunct); شوم is the 1st‑person singular of شدن 'I will become' (here future/resultative sense), and بی‌نیاز is a predicate adjective 'free of need / relieved / untroubled', so 'I shall be free of the war with the Turks.'
segments.s225-g45: ازان پس is a fixed temporal phrase 'from that time on / thereafter'; همه نیکخواه is a noun phrase 'all (are) well‑wishers'; the final token sequence written as منید is an archaic/poetic copular/ending meaning 'you are' or a verbal ending — here it functions to predicate 'you are my well‑wishers' (the separate listings of من and ید in the apparatus are morphological fragments; their ordinary listed meanings do not match this archaic copula).
segments.s225-g46: سراسر is an adverb 'entirely/throughout'; بر + آیین و راه is a locative/figurative construction 'upon (my) custom and way' meaning 'in accordance with my custom and path'; the closing منید again is the archaic copular/ending that ties the clause to the addressee(s).
segments.s225-g47: نیازم here functions as 'my plea / my supplication' or a request object ('avenge me' = 'do [for] my plea'); بکین is a poetic imperative/optative form of بکن meaning 'do / take vengeance'; و links the two clauses; نجویم (first‑person) with نبرد means 'I will not (seek) a fight' or more literally 'I will not seek battle' (نجویم is from جستن 'to seek').
segments.s225-g48: نیارم is an irregular/archaic verbal form here (the printed lemma senses do not match; in context it expresses a negated volitional result 'I will not bring' or 'I will not do [so]'); سر is the noun 'head'; سرکشان is an adjectival/agentive form 'the rebellious/insubordinate ones' (the provided lemma list does not reflect this meaning, so sense is marked unavailable); زیر گرد is a short phrase 'under the dust' (گرد here is poetic 'dust' or trampled earth) describing the fate not inflicted on those rebels.
segments.s225-g1: به خیمه درآمد = prepositional phrase + past verb: 'به' (towards/into) + 'خیمه' (tent) places the action (entered the tent) before the verb; 'درآمد' is the past tense narrative verb 'entered'. The following short adverbial phrase 'به کردار باد' uses 'به' in an adverbial sense 'in the manner of' + 'کردار' ('conduct/manner') + 'باد' ('wind') to mean 'in the manner of the wind' (i.e. like the wind).
segments.s225-g2: یکی ترگ دیگر به سر برنهاد = an enumerative clause 'one ... another ...' (یکی ... دیگر) followed by location 'به سر' (onto the head) and the past verb 'برنهاد' ('placed upon/raised onto'). The second noun phrase (دیگر) is parallel to the first; word order is noun -> locator -> verb, with 'به' here functioning as a directional/onto preposition ('onto his head').
segments.s225-g3: درفشی دگر جست و اسپی دگر = noun phrase + adjectival particle + past verb: 'درفشی دگر جست' = 'another banner/standard leapt (up)'. The conjunction 'و' links a second coordinated noun phrase 'اسپی دگر' ('another steed'), where the verb 'جست' is understood (ellipsis) for the second coordinate.
segments.s225-g4: دگرگونه جوشن دگرگون سپر = two parallel noun phrases using near-synonymous modifiers for poetic effect: 'دگرگونه جوشن' = 'a differently fashioned cuirass' (دگرگونه modifies جوشن), and 'دگرگون سپر' = 'a changed/transformed shield' (دگرگون modifies سپر). The line uses repetition and near-synonymy (دگرگونه / دگرگون) to heighten description.
segments.s225-g5: بیامد چو نزدیک رستم رسید = narrative past 'بیامد' ('he came') followed by a temporal clause introduced by 'چو' ('when'). 'نزدیک' is an adverb/adjective meaning 'near', 'رستم' names the person near whom he comes, and 'رسید' is the past verb 'reached/arrived'. The sequence gives a two-step narrative: he came, and when he grew near he arrived at Rostam.
segments.s225-g6: همی بود تا یال و شاخش بدید = 'همی بود' is the imperfective/narrative past construction meaning 'he was continuing' or 'he kept on', 'تا' introduces the extent/limit 'until', and the clause 'یال و شاخش بدید' is the event that marks that limit ('he saw his mane and horns'). Note the older past form 'بدید' (with the prefixed b-) is the simple past 'saw'.
segments.s225-g7: به رستم چنین گفت کای نامدار = indirect object first ('به رستم' = 'to Rostam'), then 'چنین گفت' = 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus'), followed by the vocative 'کای نامدار' ('O famed one'). In Persian narrative the indirect object or adverbial often precedes the verb, and direct speech follows the verb; 'کای' is a classical vocative particle.
segments.s225-g8: کمندافگن و گرد و جنگی سوار = a string of epithets in apposition to the vocative: 'کمندافگن' is a compound agent noun ('wearer/thrower of the lasso'), coordinated with 'گرد' (an archaic/poetic epithet here; exact sense uncertain) and 'جنگی سوار' ('warlike rider' — adjective + noun). Poetic coordination and omission of verbs are normal here (these are titles applied to Rostam).
segments.s225-g65: بزرگان (subject) 'the great ones/elders' is followed by a relative clause introduced by که. از تخمه ویسه‌اند literally 'are from the seed/lineage of Viseh' — تخمه functions as 'offspring/lineage' and ویسه‌اند is a nominal predicate with the 3rd‑plural ending -اند ('they are of Viseh'). Word order: 'elders who of-lineage‑Viseh‑are'.
segments.s225-g66: دو رویند is a predicate: دو + روی(ند) = 'they are two‑faced' (رویند is a plural verbal/adjectival form). The conjunction و links the next clause: با هر کسی پیسه‌اند — 'with anyone they are پیسه' where با هر کسی = 'with/for anyone' and پیسه‌اند is a predicate meaning 'are petty/insignificant' here. The verbs/adjectival predicates take the 3rd‑person plural ending -اند.
segments.s225-g67: چو here means 'like'/'such as' and introduces a list of proper names: هومان, لهاک, فرشیدورد. This is a simple enumerative clause; چو + names = 'as Hūmān and Lahāk and Farshidvard'.
segments.s225-g68: Another 'چو' ('as / like') introduces the second group: کلباد و نستیهن. آن is the demonstrative 'that' modifying شوخ مرد ('that playful/witty man'). The word order is demonstrative + adjective + noun (آن شوخ مرد = 'that witty/playful man').
segments.s225-g69: اگر این‌که introduces a condition: 'if (that) ...'. این‌که گفتم = 'what I said' (literally 'this that I said'), where گفتم functions as a relative clause 'I said'. به جای آورید is an instruction/imperative (آورید here = 'bring into place / carry out' in 2nd‑person plural or polite form).
segments.s225-g70: سر کینه جستن is a verbal nominal phrase: جستن is the infinitive/masdar 'to seek' and کینه 'hatred/grudge' is its object; سر here participates in the idiomatic phrase (→ 'the pursuit/act of vengeance/hatred'). به پای آورید is an idiom 'bring to the foot' = 'bring to an end/finish' (آورید again imperative/optative). Together: 'bring the seeking of hatred to an end.'
segments.s225-g71: ببندم is 1st‑person singular subjunctive/present ('I will bind / I shall bind'). در کینه is the prepositional phrase 'in hatred', and بر کشورت = 'against/upon your realm' (بر = 'against'). The whole unit means 'I will bind (them) in hatred against your country' (speaker declaring to fix hostility toward the addressee's land).
segments.s225-g72: This line strings short verbal pieces: بجوشن is an optative/3rd‑plural present 'let them seethe/boil' (a wish or command), نپوشید is negative imperative/form of 'پوشیدن' here 'do not cover/conceal', and باید برت = 'it is necessary/upon you' (باید = 'must', برت = 'upon you'). Grammatically it gives consecutive commands/wishes: 'let them seethe; do not conceal it — it is necessary for you.'
segments.s225-g97: This is an inverted (poetic) order of the copular clause: the verb/copula is represented by the short particle 'م' (old/poetic form of هست) placed before the object-marker 'را', so the sense is 'نامِ من است ...' — 'my name is ...'. 'کوه گوش' functions here as a compound/name (Kūh‑gūsh) and 'ای دلیر' is a vocative addressing the addressee ('O brave one').
segments.s225-g98: Standard past statement about the father: 'پدر بوسپاسست' identifies the father by name (بوسپاس is a proper name) with the -ست copula suffixed. 'مردی چو شیر' is a simile: 'a man like a lion.' Note the nominal predicate 'مردی' (a man) followed by the comparative 'چو' + 'شیر'.
segments.s225-g99: Simple past first person: 'من ... آمدم' = 'I came.' The prepositional phrase 'از وهر' ('from Vahr' — Vahr is an obscure/place name) precedes the verb (poetic word order), and 'با این سپاه' marks accompaniment/instrument ('with this army/host').
segments.s225-g100: 'سپاهی' here is a predicative/role‑noun meaning 'a soldier/warrior' (I came as a soldier). 'ب' + 'دین' is the fused prepositional phrase 'بدین' = 'to/into this', followed by 'رزمگاه' (battlefield) and the verb 'آمدم' again; the clause reads 'I came (as) a soldier into this battlefield.' Note the topical/fronted noun 'سپاهی'.
segments.s225-g101: 'ازان' = 'from that' (referring back to what was said), followed by 'باز' = 'again' and the verb 'جویم' (first‑person present/subjunctive form 'I seek' or 'I am seeking'). 'همی' is a continuous/emphatic marker ('still/continually'), and 'نام تو' is the direct object ('your name' / 'your renown'). The clause thus means 'From that, I still seek your name.'
segments.s225-g102: The conjunction 'که' introduces purpose/result ('so that' / 'in order that'). 'پیدا کنم' is a purposive clause with 'کنم' in the present/subjunctive sense ('that I may find / make apparent'). 'در جهان' = 'in the world' and 'کام تو' = 'your desire/wish' (object). Together: 'so that I may find/make your desire known in the world.'
segments.s225-g103: 'کنون‌گر' = 'if now' (کنون 'now' + گر 'if'); the conditional is formed by this particle. 'بگویی' is the 2nd person present/subjunctive ('you say / you would tell'), and 'مرا' (here split orthographically) is the indirect object 'to me' with the object-marker 'را'. 'نام خویش' = 'your name' (نام + possessive خویش). The whole condition reads 'If now you tell me your name...'.
segments.s225-g104: 'شوم' is first‑person subjunctive/future ('I shall become / I will be'), here signaling the result of the condition. 'شاد دل' is an adjectival phrase 'glad at heart'; 'سوی آرام خویش' means 'toward my own repose/peace' (سوی = toward, آرام خویش = one's own rest). The clause is therefore 'I shall be glad at heart and turn toward my own peace.'
